verses.flow: Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus through the will of God, and Timothy the brother,
verses.note: GREEK: "Apostolos" (ἀπόστολος, G652) denotes one sent with full authority, a commissioned envoy. Paul places his apostolic authority as derived "through the will of God" (διὰ θελήματος θεοῦ), establishing divine mandate before addressing the Colossian heresy.

verses.flow: To the saints and faithful brothers in Christ in Colossae: Grace to you and peace from God our Father.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: We give thanks to God, the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, always praying concerning you,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: having heard of your faith in Christ Jesus and the love which you have toward all the saints,

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: on account of the hope being laid up for you in the heavens, which you heard before in the word of the truth of the gospel,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triad of faith (v4), love (v4), and hope (v5) appears here with hope (ἐλπίς, G1680) as the foundational cause — faith and love flow from the certain hope stored in heaven. This differs from 1 Cor 13:13 where love is supreme; here the eschatological reserve of hope grounds present faithfulness.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular neuter
verb present middle participle nominative singular neuter
verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: which has come to you, just as also in all the world it is bearing fruit and growing, just as also among you from the day you heard and fully knew the grace of God in truth,
just as you learned from Epaphras, our beloved fellow-servant, who is a faithful servant of Christ on our behalf,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: who also made known to us your love in the Spirit.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: On account of this, we also, from the day we heard, do not cease praying on your behalf and asking that you might be filled with the full knowledge of his will in all wisdom and spiritual understanding,
verses.note: GREEK: "Epignōsis" (ἐπίγνωσις, G1922) is an intensified form of "gnōsis" (knowledge), meaning full, experiential, or precise knowledge. Paul deliberately uses this stronger term against the proto-gnostic claims at Colossae, asserting that true full knowledge comes through Christ alone, not through esoteric spiritual speculation.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: to walk worthily of the Lord unto all pleasing, in every good work bearing fruit and growing in the full knowledge of God,

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: being strengthened with all power according to the might of his glory, unto all endurance and longsuffering with joy,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: giving thanks to the Father who made you sufficient for the share of the inheritance of the saints in the light,
who delivered us out of the authority of the darkness and transferred us into the kingdom of the Son of his love,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "The authority of the darkness" (ἐξουσία τοῦ σκότους) refers to the ruling power of the domain of hostile spiritual beings. God's act of "transferring" (μεθίστημι, G3179) believers into Christ's kingdom echoes the divine council motif where God reassigns the nations from rebellious elohim (Deut 32:8-9 LXX) to the rule of his Son. The "kingdom of the Son of his love" stands in direct contrast to the dominion of fallen powers.

verses.flow: in whom we have the redemption, the forgiveness of sins,
who is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Image" (εἰκών, G1504) echoes Gen 1:26-27 LXX — Christ is the perfect and original image of the invisible God that Adam was meant to reflect. "Firstborn of all creation" (πρωτότοκος πάσης κτίσεως) denotes supremacy and preeminence of rank, not temporal origin, as v16 immediately clarifies: all things were created in him. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the true "image of God" fulfills and surpasses the Adamic vocation (Gen 1:26; 2 Cor 4:4).

verses.flow: for in him were created all things in the heavens and upon the earth, the visible and the invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities — all things through him and unto him have been created,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The list "thrones, dominions, rulers, authorities" (θρόνοι, κυριότητες, ἀρχαί, ἐξουσίαι) names ranks within the heavenly hierarchy — both loyal and rebellious spiritual beings. All were created in, through, and unto Christ. This directly counters Colossian angel-veneration by subordinating every heavenly power to Christ's creative sovereignty.

verses.flow: and he himself is before all things, and in him all things hold together,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and he himself is the head of the body, the assembly; who is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead, so that in all things he himself might have the preeminence,
verses.note: GREEK: "Prōteuōn" (πρωτεύων, G4409) is a NT hapax legomenon, meaning "holding first place" or "having preeminence." Combined with "archē" (beginning/origin) and "firstborn from the dead," Paul establishes Christ's absolute supremacy in both creation and new creation. TYPOLOGY: As firstborn from the dead, Christ inaugurates the eschatological resurrection, fulfilling the firstfruits typology (cf. 1 Cor 15:20).

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: for in him all the fullness was pleased to dwell,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Plērōma" (πλήρωμα, G4138) — "fullness" — is a key term Paul likely reclaims from proto-gnostic vocabulary at Colossae. Rather than the divine fullness being distributed among emanations or aeons, all the fullness was pleased to permanently dwell in Christ alone. The verb "eudokeō" (was pleased) implies the Father's sovereign will and delight in this arrangement.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and through him to reconcile all things unto him, having made peace through the blood of his cross — through him — whether the things upon the earth or the things in the heavens.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Apokatallassō" (ἀποκαταλλάσσω, G604) is a double-compounded verb unique to Paul (also Eph 2:16), intensifying the concept of reconciliation. The scope is cosmic — "all things" whether on earth or in the heavens — accomplished through the blood of the cross. This cosmic reconciliation includes the subjugation of hostile powers and the restoration of all creation under Christ's headship (cf. Eph 1:10).

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And you, being once alienated and enemies in the mind by wicked works,
yet now he has reconciled in the body of his flesh through death — to present you holy and unblemished and blameless before him,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle genitive singular neuter

verses.flow: if indeed you continue in the faith, having been founded and firm, and not being moved away from the hope of the gospel which you heard, which was proclaimed in all creation under heaven, of which I, Paul, became a servant.
Now I rejoice in my sufferings on your behalf, and I fill up in my flesh what is lacking of the afflictions of Christ on behalf of his body, which is the assembly,
verses.note: GREEK: "Antanaplēroō" (ἀνταναπληρόω, G466) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "to fill up in return what is lacking." Paul does not suggest Christ's atoning work is incomplete, but that the apostolic vocation of suffering for the sake of the church-body participates in the ongoing pattern of Christ's afflictions (θλίψεις, not his sacrificial death). THEOLOGICAL: This distinguishes redemptive suffering (complete in Christ alone) from ministerial suffering (the apostle's share in proclaiming and embodying the gospel).

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: of which I became a servant according to the stewardship of God given to me for you, to fulfill the word of God,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: the mystery having been hidden from the ages and from the generations — but now was made manifest to his saints,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Mystērion" (μυστήριον, G3466) denotes not an unsolvable puzzle but a divine plan once hidden and now revealed. The mystery hidden from the ages and generations has a specific content revealed in v27: Christ among the nations. This disclosure marks the eschatological turning point — God's secret counsel for Gentile inclusion is now made manifest to his saints (cf. Eph 3:4-6; Rom 16:25-26).

verses.flow: to whom God willed to make known what is the riches of the glory of this mystery among the nations, which is Christ in you, the hope of glory,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Christ in you, the hope of glory" is the apex of the mystery — the indwelling presence of Christ among the Gentile nations fulfills the OT promises that God's glory would extend to all peoples (Isa 49:6; Ps 72:19). The phrase also echoes the tabernacle/temple motif: God's glorious presence now dwells not in a structure but in his people through the Messiah.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: whom we proclaim, admonishing every man and teaching every man in all wisdom, so that we might present every man complete in Christ,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: unto which I also labor, striving according to his working, the one working in me in power.
For I desire you to know how great a struggle I have on behalf of you and of those in Laodicea, and as many as have not seen my face in the flesh,

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: that their hearts may be comforted, having been knit together in love, and unto all the riches of the full assurance of understanding, unto the knowledge of the mystery of God, that is, Christ,
verses.note: GREEK: "Mystery of God, that is, Christ" (τοῦ μυστηρίου τοῦ θεοῦ, Χριστοῦ) — the textual tradition varies here, but the best reading makes Christ himself the content of God's mystery. "Symbibasthentes" (συμβιβασθέντες, Strong's G4822) means "knit together, united," carrying the sense of being instructed and compacted into unity. THEOLOGICAL: The "full assurance of understanding" (πληροφορία τῆς συνέσεως) stands in deliberate contrast to the false "philosophy" warned against in v.8—true knowledge is found exclusively in Christ (Eph 3:3-4).

verses.flow: in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and of knowledge.
This I say, that no one may deceive you with persuasive speech.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For even if in the flesh I am absent, yet in the spirit I am with you, rejoicing and beholding your order and the firmness of your faith toward Christ.
As therefore you received Christ Jesus the Lord, in him walk,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: having been rooted and being built up in him, and being established in the faith, just as you were taught, abounding in thanksgiving.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Beware lest there shall be anyone who takes you captive through philosophy and empty deceit, according to the tradition of men, according to the elemental powers of the world, and not according to Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "Sylagōgōn" (συλαγωγῶν, Strong's G4812) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "to carry off as plunder/spoil," depicting false teachers as spiritual kidnappers. "Pithanologia" (πιθανολογία, v.4, G4086) is also a hapax. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Stoicheia tou kosmou" (στοιχεῖα τοῦ κόσμου) — "elemental powers of the world" — likely refers not merely to basic principles but to spiritual powers or cosmic entities that were understood in Second Temple Judaism to govern the nations and the material order (cf. Gal 4:3, 9; Deut 32:8 LXX). Paul contrasts these subordinate powers with the supreme authority of Christ.

words.g: which is put in to fill up

verses.flow: For in him dwells all the fullness of the Deity bodily,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Theotēs" (θεότητος, Strong's G2320) is a NT hapax distinct from "theiotēs" (Rom 1:20). Theotēs denotes the very essence and being of God—not merely divine qualities but the fullness of absolute Deity. "Sōmatikōs" (bodily) affirms that this divine fullness dwells in Christ in embodied, incarnate form—permanently, not temporarily. This is among the strongest Christological affirmations in the NT (cf. John 1:14; Col 1:19).

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and you are in him having been made full, who is the head of every ruler and authority,
in whom also you were circumcised with a circumcision not made with hands, in the putting off of the body of the flesh, in the circumcision of Christ,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "circumcision not made with hands" fulfills the OT promise of the circumcision of the heart (Deut 30:6; Jer 4:4). The "putting off of the body of the flesh" (τῇ ἀπεκδύσει τοῦ σώματος τῆς σαρκός) echoes the stripping imagery used of the hostile powers in v.15, linking the believer's liberation to Christ's victory. GREEK: "Acheiropoiētos" (ἀχειροποίητος, G886) — "not made with hands" — contrasts the divine action with all human religious ritual (cf. Mark 14:58; 2 Cor 5:1).

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: having been buried together with him in baptism, in which also you were raised together through the faith of the working of God, who raised him from the dead.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And you, being dead in your trespasses and the uncircumcision of your flesh, he made alive together with him, having forgiven us all our trespasses,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: having blotted out the handwriting in ordinances that was against us, which was contrary to us; and he has taken it out of the midst, having nailed it to the cross.
verses.note: GREEK: "Cheirographon" (χειρόγραφον, G5498) is a NT hapax meaning a handwritten document of debt — a legal certificate of indebtedness. "Dogmasin" (δόγμασιν) refers to the ordinances or decrees. THEOLOGICAL: The debt-document representing our obligation under the law is not merely canceled but nailed to the cross — a dramatic image of Christ's substitutionary death annulling the legal claim that stood against humanity (cf. Eph 2:14-15). TYPOLOGY: The nailing to the cross transforms the Roman instrument of condemnation into the instrument of liberation.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Having stripped the rulers and the authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them in it.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Archas kai tas exousias" (τὰς ἀρχὰς καὶ τὰς ἐξουσίας) — "the rulers and the authorities" — refers to hostile spiritual powers in the divine council framework, not merely human rulers. Christ's cross is paradoxically his enthronement: the moment of apparent defeat is the moment of cosmic victory. "Apekdysamenos" (ἀπεκδυσάμενος, G554) means "having stripped/disarmed," portraying Christ as the divine warrior who despoils his enemies. "Thriambeusas" (θριαμβεύσας) evokes the Roman triumphal procession, displaying conquered enemies publicly (cf. 2 Cor 2:14).

verses.flow: Therefore let no one judge you in eating or in drinking, or in respect of a feast or a new moon or sabbaths,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural neuter

verses.flow: which things are a shadow of the things to come, but the body is of Christ.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The festivals, new moons, and sabbaths are declared to be a "shadow" (σκιά) of the coming realities, while the "body" (σῶμα) — the substance casting the shadow — belongs to Christ. This shadow-substance typology parallels Hebrews 8:5 and 10:1. The entire Israelite liturgical calendar finds its fulfillment and substance in the person and work of Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Let no one disqualify you, delighting in humility and worship of the angels, entering into things he has seen, vainly being puffed up by the mind of his flesh,
verses.note: GREEK: "Katabrabeuetō" (καταβραβευέτω, G2603) is a NT hapax meaning "to decide against as an umpire, to disqualify, to rob of a prize" — a term from athletic competition. "Thrēskeia tōn angelōn" (θρησκείᾳ τῶν ἀγγέλων) could be either subjective genitive ("worship that angels perform") or objective genitive ("worship directed toward angels"). DIVINE_COUNCIL: In Second Temple Jewish mystical tradition, angelic liturgy and visionary ascent practices were known (cf. Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice from Qumran). "Embateuōn" (ἐμβατεύων, G1687) likely refers to entering a heavenly sanctuary in visionary experience. Paul warns that such practices, disconnected from Christ the Head, are products of fleshly presumption.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: and not holding fast the Head, from whom all the body, being supplied and being knit together through the joints and ligaments, grows with the growth of God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: If you died with Christ from the elemental powers of the world, why, as though living in the world, do you submit to decrees:
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Apethanete syn Christō" — "you died with Christ" — the believer's co-crucifixion with Christ severs the binding authority of the stoicheia (elemental powers). "Dogmatizesthe" (δογματίζεσθε, G1379) is a NT hapax meaning "to submit to decrees/regulations." The rhetorical question exposes the absurdity of those who, having been freed from cosmic powers through union with Christ's death, voluntarily re-enslave themselves to human religious regulations (cf. Gal 4:9).

verses.flow: "Do not handle, nor taste, nor touch"—
which things are all destined for corruption in their consumption—according to the commandments and teachings of men?

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter

verses.flow: Which things indeed have a reputation of wisdom in self-imposed worship and humility and severe treatment of the body, but are not of any value against the indulgence of the flesh.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ethelothrēskia" (ἐθελοθρησκία, G1479) is a NT hapax meaning "self-imposed worship" or "would-be religion" — worship that originates in human will rather than divine command. "Apheidia sōmatos" (ἀφειδίᾳ σώματος) — "severe treatment of the body" — another rare term (G857, hapax). THEOLOGICAL: Paul's conclusion is devastating: all such ascetic regulations possess an appearance (λόγον) of wisdom but have no actual value (οὐκ ἐν τιμῇ τινι) in restraining fleshly indulgence. External religion without Christ is powerless against the flesh.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: If then you were raised together with Christ, seek the things above, where Christ is, at the right hand of God sitting.
verses.note: GREEK: "Synēgerthēte" (συνηγέρθητε, Strong's G4891) is a compound verb: σύν (with) + ἐγείρω (raise). The aorist passive indicates a completed past event — believers' co-resurrection with Christ is already accomplished in the divine economy. "Ta anō" (the things above) contrasts the heavenly realm with the earthly throughout vv. 1-4. THEOLOGICAL: Christ seated at God's right hand (Ps 110:1) establishes his cosmic kingship and the spatial orientation of the believer's new allegiance.

verses.flow: The things above set your mind on, not the things upon the earth.
For you died, and your life has been hidden with Christ in God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Apethanete" (you died) is aorist — a definitive past event linked to baptism (Col 2:12, 20). "Kekryptai" (κέκρυπται, G2928) is perfect passive — your life has been hidden and remains hidden. The hiddenness of the believer's true life in God provides both protection and mystery: the fullness of what believers are awaits eschatological unveiling (v. 4). TYPOLOGY: The hidden life echoes the theme of God concealing his people — as Israel was hidden in Egypt, so the church's life is concealed with Christ in the heavenly realm.

verses.flow: When Christ is made manifest, your life, then also you with him shall be made manifest in glory.
verses.note: GREEK: "Phanerōthē" (φανερωθῇ, G5319) — "made manifest, revealed" — is used twice in this verse, first of Christ's appearing and then of believers' co-manifestation. This is not merely "appearing" but an uncovering of hidden reality. THEOLOGICAL: This is a key eschatological text: the parousia of Christ will simultaneously reveal his glory and the glorified identity of his people (Rom 8:18-19; 1 John 3:2).

verses.flow: Put to death therefore the members that are upon the earth: sexual immorality, uncleanness, passion, evil desire, and covetousness, which is idolatry,
verses.note: GREEK: "Nekrōsate" (νεκρώσατε, G3499) is an aorist imperative — a decisive, once-for-all putting to death. The list moves from outward acts (porneía, sexual immorality) to inward dispositions (evil desire) to root sin (pleonexía, covetousness). THEOLOGICAL: Paul identifies covetousness as "idolatry" (εἰδωλολατρία), linking disordered desire to worship of false gods — a striking connection between the moral and the cultic (Eph 5:5).

verses.flow: on account of which things comes the wrath of God upon the sons of disobedience,
in which also you once walked, when you were living in these things.
But now put away also you all these things: wrath, fury, malice, blasphemy, filthy speech out of your mouth.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Do not lie to one another, having stripped off the old man with his practices,

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle accusative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: and having put on the new man, the one being renewed unto full knowledge according to the image of the one having created him,
verses.note: GREEK: "Anakainoumenon" (ἀνακαινούμενον, G341) is a present passive participle — the new man is being continuously renewed, indicating an ongoing process. "Kat' eikona tou ktisantos" (according to the image of the one having created him) is a direct allusion to Gen 1:26-27 (LXX). TYPOLOGY: The new creation in Christ restores the image of God marred in Adam. The "full knowledge" (epignōsis, G1922) toward which renewal moves is not mere information but relational, participatory knowing of God.

verses.flow: where there is not Greek and Jew, circumcision and uncircumcision, barbarian, Scythian, bondservant, free, but Christ is all things and in all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse abolishes all ethnic, religious, cultural, and social distinctions as ultimate categories within the new humanity. "Christ is all things and in all" (ta panta kai en pasin Christos) is a stunning claim of Christ's totality — he fills every category and inhabits every member. HISTORICAL: The inclusion of "Scythian" is remarkable — Scythians were regarded as the lowest of barbarians in Greco-Roman estimation, yet even this distinction is dissolved in Christ.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Put on therefore, as chosen ones of God, holy and beloved, bowels of compassion, kindness, humility of mind, meekness, longsuffering,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: bearing with one another and forgiving yourselves, if anyone against anyone should have a complaint; even as also the Lord forgave you, so also you.
And upon all these things, love, which is the bond of perfection.
verses.note: GREEK: "Syndesmos tēs teleiotētos" (σύνδεσμος τῆς τελειότητος) — "the bond of perfection." Syndesmos (G4886) is that which binds together; teleiotēs (G5047) denotes completeness or maturity. Love (agapē) is the binding force that brings all the virtues of vv. 12-13 to their completion. THEOLOGICAL: Love is not merely one virtue among many but the integrating principle that perfects the whole — the capstone of the new humanity's moral life.

verses.flow: And let the peace of Christ rule in your hearts, unto which also you were called in one body; and be thankful.
verses.note: GREEK: "Brabeuetō" (βραβευέτω, G1018) means "let it act as umpire" or "let it rule as arbiter" — drawn from athletic contest language where a brabeus adjudicated the prize. This is a NT hapax legomenon. The peace of Christ is to be the decisive arbitrating authority in the hearts of believers and in community disputes. THEOLOGICAL: "In one body" (en heni sōmati) grounds the imperative ecclesiologically — peace rules because believers share one corporate body in Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, in all wisdom teaching and admonishing one another with psalms, hymns, spiritual songs, in grace singing in your hearts to God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And all things, whatever you do in word or in deed, do all things in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him.
Wives, submit yourselves to your husbands, as is fitting in the Lord.
Husbands, love your wives, and do not be embittered against them.
Children, obey your parents in all things, for this is well-pleasing in the Lord.
Fathers, do not provoke your children, lest they become disheartened.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Bondservants, obey in all things your masters according to the flesh, not with eye-service as men-pleasers, but in sincerity of heart, fearing the Lord.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ophthalmodouliais" (ὀφθαλμοδουλίαις, G3787) — "eye-service" — is a rare compound word, possibly coined by Paul, meaning service performed only when being watched. "Haplotēti kardias" (sincerity of heart) stands in contrast: an undivided, single-focused heart. HISTORICAL: The household codes (Haustafel, vv. 18-4:1) address the Roman household structure. Paul subverts its logic by directing the bondservant's ultimate allegiance to the Lord Christ, not the earthly master.

verses.flow: Whatever you do, work from the soul, as to the Lord and not to men,

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: knowing that from the Lord you shall receive the recompense of the inheritance. The Lord Christ you serve.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Tō kyriō Christō douleuete" — "The Lord Christ you serve" — may be read as indicative (you are serving) or imperative (serve!). Either way, it redefines servitude: the true Master of all bondservants is the Lord Christ. "Antapodosin tēs klēronomias" (the recompense of the inheritance) is remarkable — bondservants, who legally could inherit nothing in Roman law, are promised a divine inheritance. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Israel's inheritance of the promised land, now universalized as an eschatological reward from the Lord.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the one doing wrong shall receive back what he did wrongly, and there is no partiality.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Masters, that which is just and equal provide to your bondservants, knowing that you also have a Master in heaven.
verses.note: GREEK: κύριοι (kyrioi, Strong's G2962) here means "masters" in the social sense, while κύριον (kyrion) in the second clause refers to Christ as heavenly Master. The wordplay is deliberate — earthly masters are accountable to the ultimate Lord. ἰσότητα (isotēta, G2471) means "equality" or "fairness," a remarkable term in a slaveholding context. THEOLOGICAL: Paul subverts the master-slave hierarchy by establishing Christ's lordship over all human authority structures (Col 3:24-25; Eph 6:9).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: In prayer continue steadfastly, being watchful in it with thanksgiving,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: praying at the same time also for us, that God may open to us a door of the word, to speak the mystery of Christ, on account of which I have also been bound,
verses.note: GREEK: μυστήριον (mystērion, G3466) is a key Pauline term in Colossians, referring not to something unknowable but to God's previously hidden plan now revealed in Christ (Col 1:26-27; Eph 3:3-6). θύραν τοῦ λόγου ("door of the word") is a metaphor for gospel opportunity (cf. 1 Cor 16:9; 2 Cor 2:12). δέδεμαι (dedemai, perfect passive of δέω) indicates Paul's ongoing state of imprisonment at the time of writing.

verses.flow: that I may make it manifest, as it is necessary for me to speak.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: In wisdom walk toward those who are outside, redeeming the time.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐξαγοραζόμενοι (exagorazomenoi, G1805) literally means "buying up" or "redeeming" the time (καιρόν, kairon — opportune season, not mere chronological time). The middle voice suggests personal investment. τοὺς ἔξω ("those outside") is a standard designation for non-believers (cf. 1 Cor 5:12-13; 1 Thess 4:12). THEOLOGICAL: Wisdom toward outsiders reflects the missionary posture of the early assembly.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Let your word be always in grace, seasoned with salt, so as to know how it is necessary for you to answer each one.
verses.note: GREEK: ἅλατι ἠρτυμένος ("seasoned with salt") — ἀρτύω (artyō, G741) means to season or prepare food. Salt (hálas, G217) signifies both preservation and flavor. This parallels Jesus' teaching on salt in Matt 5:13 and Mark 9:50. THEOLOGICAL: Christian speech must be simultaneously gracious (ἐν χάριτι) and substantive, not bland — able to meet each individual's need.

verses.flow: All the things concerning me Tychicus shall make known to you, the beloved brother and faithful servant and fellow-bondservant in the Lord,
whom I sent to you for this very purpose, that you may know the things concerning us and that he may encourage your hearts,
together with Onesimus, the faithful and beloved brother, who is from among you. All things here they shall make known to you.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Onesimus (Ὀνήσιμος, G3682, meaning "useful" or "profitable") is almost certainly the same runaway bondservant commended in Paul's epistle to Philemon. The phrase ὅς ἐστιν ἐξ ὑμῶν ("who is from among you") identifies him as a Colossian, linking this letter directly to Philemon's household. His designation as "faithful and beloved brother" publicly elevates his status before the entire assembly.

verses.flow: Aristarchus my fellow-prisoner greets you, and Mark the cousin of Barnabas (concerning whom you received commandments — if he comes to you, receive him),

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and Jesus who is called Justus — these alone of the circumcision are fellow-workers for the kingdom of God, who have been a comfort to me.
verses.note: GREEK: παρηγορία (parēgoria, G3931) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "comfort" or "consolation." It was used in medical literature for soothing remedies. HISTORICAL: "These alone of the circumcision" indicates that only these three Jewish believers — Aristarchus, Mark, and Justus — were actively collaborating with Paul for the kingdom. The phrase βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ ("kingdom of God") is rare in Paul's prison epistles, underscoring the royal-eschatological dimension of their mission.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Epaphras greets you, who is from among you, a bondservant of Christ, always striving on your behalf in his prayers, that you may stand mature and fully assured in all the will of God.
For I bear witness concerning him that he has much toil on behalf of you and those in Laodicea and those in Hierapolis.
Luke the beloved physician greets you, and Demas.
Greet the brothers in Laodicea, and Nympha and the assembly in her house.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Νύμφαν (Nymphan) with the pronoun αὐτῆς ("her") indicates a woman hosting a house-assembly in Laodicea. Some manuscripts read αὐτοῦ ("his"), reflecting later scribal discomfort with female ecclesial leadership. THEOLOGICAL: The κατ' οἶκον ἐκκλησίαν ("assembly according to house") reflects the standard structure of early Christian worship before dedicated buildings existed (cf. Rom 16:5; 1 Cor 16:19; Phlm 1:2).

verses.flow: And when this epistle has been read among you, cause that it be read also in the assembly of the Laodiceans, and that you also read the one from Laodicea.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: τὴν ἐκ Λαοδικείας ("the one from Laodicea") refers to an epistle circulating from Laodicea — its identity is debated. Some have identified it with Ephesians (which may have been a circular letter) or a now-lost Pauline letter. This instruction reveals the early practice of reading apostolic letters aloud in assembled worship and sharing them among neighboring congregations, anticipating the eventual canonical collection.

verses.flow: And say to Archippus, "See to the ministry which you received in the Lord, that you fulfill it."
The greeting by my own hand — Paul. Remember my bonds. Grace be with you.
verses.note: GREEK: τῇ ἐμῇ χειρί ("by my own hand") indicates Paul took the pen from his amanuensis to write the closing himself, authenticating the letter (cf. 1 Cor 16:21; Gal 6:11; 2 Thess 3:17). μνημονεύετέ μου τῶν δεσμῶν ("remember my bonds") — δεσμῶν (desmōn, G1199) denotes literal chains, a poignant final request connecting to the μυστήριον for which he is bound (4:3). The abrupt brevity of this closing is striking compared to other Pauline epistles.
